name: AI Foundations · Beginner
description: AI & machine-learning basics for beginners — no experience needed.

questions.text: How does machine learning differ from traditional programming?
options: It runs the same fixed rules much faster
It writes documentation for existing computer programs
It needs no computer to run its logic
It learns patterns from data instead of coded rules
questions.explanation: In ML you provide examples and the model infers the rules, rather than a developer coding every rule explicitly.

options: Only images, never text or numbers
Random noise generated during training
Labelled examples pairing inputs with known outputs
Unlabelled data with no correct answers given at all
questions.explanation: The model learns to map inputs to outputs from examples where the answer is already known.

options: Only small, perfectly clean datasets
Unlabelled data, finding structure on its own
Labelled data with a known target per row
Human feedback given after every prediction
questions.explanation: With no labels, it discovers patterns such as clusters or associations in the data.

options: Running the finished model in production
Deleting old data the model no longer needs
Adjusting the model on data to improve a task
Writing the documentation for the model
questions.explanation: Training tunes the model's internal parameters to reduce its error on the training data.

options: Using a trained model to predict on new input
Cleaning and formatting the raw dataset
Gathering and labelling the training data
Adjusting the model's weights during training
questions.explanation: Inference is the model doing its job on fresh, unseen inputs after training is done.

options: Fits the training data too closely, generalises poorly
Has far too few parameters to be useful
Runs out of GPU memory partway through the training run
Trains for too few steps to learn anything
questions.explanation: An overfit model has essentially memorised the training set, so it performs badly on data it hasn't seen.

questions.text: Why split data into training and test sets?
options: To let the model train on twice as much data
To gauge performance honestly on unseen data
To save disk space while storing the dataset
It is optional and rarely makes any difference
questions.explanation: Evaluating on held-out data prevents you from being fooled by performance on examples the model already learned.

options: Classification predicts categories; regression numbers
Both predict a continuous numeric value
Regression predicts categories; classification numbers
Both are only ever used on image data
questions.explanation: Predicting 'spam/not spam' is classification; predicting a house price is regression.

options: An input variable the model uses to predict
The final prediction the model produces
The loop that repeatedly updates the weights
A defect or bug in the training pipeline
questions.explanation: Features are the measurable inputs (e.g. age, pixels, words) the model bases its output on.

questions.text: At its core, a large language model…
options: Performs only arithmetic and no language tasks
Understands language exactly as a human does
Predicts the likely next tokens of text
Looks each answer up in a fixed database
questions.explanation: An LLM is trained to continue text by predicting the most probable next token given what came before.

options: A password used to access the model's hosted API
The dataset the model was trained upon
A chunk of text, a word or subword, it processes
The graphics card the model runs on
questions.explanation: Models break text into tokens; both input length and output are measured in tokens.

options: A particular type of graphics processing unit or chip
A crash that stops the model responding
A response that arrives unusually slowly
Confident output that is factually wrong or made up
questions.explanation: Models can produce fluent, plausible-sounding text that isn't true — always verify important facts.

questions.text: Why can biased training data be a problem?
options: It reduces how much memory training needs
It has no measurable effect on the outputs
It makes the model train noticeably faster
The model learns and can amplify that bias
questions.explanation: A model reflects its data; skewed or unfair data leads to skewed or unfair predictions.

questions.text: 'Garbage in, garbage out' in ML means…
options: Poor-quality data leads to a poor model
The output is always meaningless regardless
The quality of the data does not matter
The model automatically deletes any bad data
questions.explanation: Model quality is bounded by data quality; noisy or wrong data produces an unreliable model.

options: Building a brand-new model entirely from scratch
Deleting parameters to shrink a large model
Running a finished model to make predictions
Further training a pretrained model on new data
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning adapts an existing general model to a narrower task or domain with additional training.

options: The prompts a user types at inference
The learned weights adjusted during training
The hardware the model happens to run on
The input files fed into the model
questions.explanation: Parameters are the numbers the model learns; large models have billions of them.

questions.text: Why are GPUs commonly used for ML?
options: They can store far more data than a CPU
They are legally required for training models
They do the parallel matrix maths far faster
They are considerably cheaper than any CPU
questions.explanation: Training and inference are mostly large matrix operations, which GPUs execute massively in parallel.

options: A password protecting access to the data
A vector that captures meaning and similarity
An image generated from the input text
A single number summarising the whole input
questions.explanation: Embeddings map items into a vector space where similar things sit close together, enabling search and comparison.

questions.text: Why is accuracy alone misleading on imbalanced data?
options: It always needs a GPU to be measured at all
Imbalanced data makes every model fully accurate
A model can score high by always guessing the majority
Accuracy simply cannot be computed on imbalanced data
questions.explanation: If 99% of cases are negative, always predicting 'negative' scores 99% while catching nothing useful.

options: The model develops genuine human emotions
The model is left to act with no oversight
People review or approve the model's outputs
Training data is entered entirely by hand
questions.explanation: Keeping a human checkpoint helps catch errors and handle high-stakes or ambiguous decisions.

options: How accurate the model's answers tend to be
How much text the model can consider at once
How much the model costs to run per request
The size of the graphics card it runs on
questions.explanation: The context window limits how much prompt and conversation the model can take into account in one go.

questions.text: Increasing the 'temperature' when generating text tends to…
options: Makes the generated output more random and varied
Makes the model respond noticeably faster
Reliably improves the factual accuracy
Lowers the compute cost of each generated response
questions.explanation: Higher temperature increases randomness; lower temperature makes output more focused and deterministic.

questions.text: Why validate AI-generated code before running it?
options: Running it first is the only way to read it
It can be subtly wrong or insecure despite looking right
Generated code is always flawless and perfectly safe to run
It only ever fails to compile, never at runtime
questions.explanation: Generated code may contain bugs or vulnerabilities that read plausibly — review and test it like any code.

questions.text: Why is pasting secrets or customer data into an external AI tool risky?
options: The data leaves your control and may be retained
It merely makes the request run a little slower
It trains a private model just for your team
It slightly increases the cost of the request
questions.explanation: Sensitive data sent to a third party may be retained or leaked; follow your org's data-handling rules.

options: Nothing; they are strictly better everywhere
More data needed but far cheaper hardware
Lower quality in exchange for much faster replies
Higher cost and latency for potentially better quality
questions.explanation: Larger models can be more capable but cost more and respond slower; pick the right size for the job.

options: Written and stored in advance by a human
Pure random noise with no useful structure
Identical every single time for a given prompt
Probabilistic — the same prompt can vary
questions.explanation: Generation samples from probabilities, so responses can vary run to run unless settings force determinism.

options: The answer the model returns to you
The dataset originally used to train the model
The input or instructions you give the model
The graphics card the model runs on
questions.explanation: The prompt is what you send in; clear, specific prompts generally get better results.

options: Making the underlying model much larger
Supplying relevant documents to ground the answer
Removing the prompt from the request entirely
Retraining the model from scratch each time
questions.explanation: RAG fetches relevant text and feeds it to the model so answers can cite up-to-date, specific sources.

questions.text: Reinforcement learning trains an agent using…
options: No data or feedback of any kind at all
Only human-written text as its sole input
Labelled images paired with their correct answers
Rewards and penalties shaped by the agent's actions
questions.explanation: The agent learns a strategy by trying actions and being rewarded for good outcomes.

questions.text: Saying a model is 'not sentient' means it…
options: Produces learned patterns, without understanding or intent
Means the model is currently broken, offline or unreachable
Means it has genuine beliefs and awareness
Means it cannot run without a human present
questions.explanation: However fluent, the model has no beliefs or awareness; it's statistical pattern completion.

questions.text: When are simple hand-written rules often better than ML?
options: Never; machine learning is always the better choice
When the logic is well-defined and stable
Always, regardless of the problem at hand
Only when working exclusively with image data
questions.explanation: If you can state the rules exactly, code them — ML shines when patterns are complex or hard to specify.

questions.text: Why is transparency/explainability valued in AI decisions?
options: Because it lets you skip testing the model before release
Because it reduces the cost of running it
Because it makes the model respond faster
So outcomes can be understood, trusted and challenged
questions.explanation: Especially for decisions affecting people, being able to explain why a model decided something supports trust and accountability.
